findings.title: vuedraggable mutates recentClosed/olderClosed directly, causing desync with props.applications on next watch flush
findings.why_it_matters: vuedraggable 4.x mutates its v-model array when an item is dragged out of the list (it splices the element out). recentClosed and olderClosed are refs populated by the watch on props.applications. When the user drags a card from recentClosed to an active column, vuedraggable removes the element from recentClosed.value. The @change handler fires, emits status-change, which triggers handleStatusChange → refreshApplication → applications.value is updated → watch re-runs partitionClosed → recentClosed.value is reassigned. So far so good IF the watch is synchronous. However, between the drag-end and the async network call completing, recentClosed.value has already been mutated by vuedraggable. If the network call fails or if two drags happen in quick succession, recentClosed ends up in an inconsistent state (card removed optimistically but server state unchanged). More critically: olderClosed is also a separate v-model. If a card is dragged from olderClosed to recentClosed (both are in the same drag group='applications'), vuedraggable updates both arrays optimistically. onClosedChange fires with evt.added containing an app whose status is already terminal. The guard `if (!TERMINAL_STAGES.includes(app.status))` prevents emitting a status-change. The visual state now has the card in recentClosed, but the next watch re-run from any unrelated update will re-partition and move it back. No data corruption occurs, but the UI can flicker or appear to undo the drag.
findings.evidence: KanbanBoard.vue lines 60-93: two separate draggable components share group='applications'. recentClosed (line 61) and olderClosed (line 83) are independent refs. onClosedChange (line 294-301) only emits when status is not terminal, meaning intra-closed drags silently mutate the local arrays without emitting. The watch at line 268 reassigns both refs whenever props.applications changes, overwriting any local vuedraggable mutations.
findings.suggested_fix: For drags within the closed column (accepted↔rejected or recentClosed↔olderClosed), emit a proper status-change if the target bucket implies a different status (e.g. dragging into olderClosed should still be treated as same status, but dragging accepted→rejected bucket should emit). Alternatively, make recentClosed/olderClosed computed (readonly) and use a single columns.accepted/columns.rejected as the v-model, letting the watch re-partition after each server round-trip. The safest fix: merge recentClosed and olderClosed into a single closedApps ref used as v-model, and show the 'older' section as a visual filter only (not a separate draggable list). This eliminates the split-ref mutation problem.

findings.title: onClosedChange assigns 'rejected' to accepted cards dragged within the Closed column
findings.why_it_matters: The Closed column renders two draggable lists (recentClosed, olderClosed) both with group='applications'. Both use onClosedChange as their @change handler. If a user drags an 'accepted' card from one list to the other (e.g. from olderClosed into recentClosed), vuedraggable fires evt.added on the destination list with the accepted card. The guard `if (!TERMINAL_STAGES.includes(app.status))` is true for 'accepted', so the condition is FALSE — meaning no emit happens. That is the correct behaviour for intra-closed drags (no status change needed). However, if an active-stage card is dragged into the closed column for the first time, it is assigned 'rejected' regardless of whether the user intended 'accepted'. This is a UX concern rather than a data-loss bug, but an accepted app being silently reassigned to rejected on drag is a correctness issue. Additionally, both active-stage draggable columns (desktop activeStages loop and mobile mobileClosedGroup stages) use columns.accepted/columns.rejected as v-model via the generic onChange handler. Those handlers correctly emit the specific target status. But the desktop Closed column uses recentClosed/olderClosed (not columns.accepted/columns.rejected) so 'accepted' and 'rejected' are now treated as an undifferentiated pool — dragging into either list always maps to 'rejected'.
findings.evidence: KanbanBoard.vue line 294-301: onClosedChange emits 'rejected' for any non-terminal card. The closed column mixes accepted and rejected cards in both recentClosed and olderClosed (partitionClosed at line 251 does not sort by sub-status). Mobile uses columns.accepted/columns.rejected (line 168-179) via onChange which correctly maps to the specific column status. Desktop does not.
findings.suggested_fix: Either (a) remove the desktop drag-into-closed capability entirely (make the closed column drop-target-free, since dragging an active card to 'closed' is an unusual action and the correct terminal status is ambiguous), or (b) split the desktop closed column into separate 'Accepted' and 'Rejected' draggable lists each with their own v-model (columns.accepted / columns.rejected) and use the same onChange(evt, 'accepted') / onChange(evt, 'rejected') pattern used everywhere else.

findings.title: pulseTimeout is module-level and never cleared on component unmount
findings.why_it_matters: App.vue is a singleton in practice, so this is low-risk in normal usage. However, it is a correctness issue: if the component is unmounted (e.g. during HMR hot-reload in development, or in a test harness), the setTimeout callback fires against a destroyed component instance, calling pulseGhost.value = false on a ref that no longer drives a live component. In HMR this manifests as a harmless no-op, but the lack of cleanup is a pattern that can cause harder-to-debug issues if the architecture changes. The `pulseTimeout` variable is declared at module scope (outside setup), so it is shared across all instances if the component were ever instantiated more than once.
findings.evidence: App.vue line 195: `let pulseTimeout = null` declared at module scope. Line 287-290: setTimeout callback sets pulseGhost.value. No onUnmounted cleanup exists.
findings.suggested_fix: Move pulseTimeout declaration inside the <script setup> block (making it instance-scoped). Add `onUnmounted(() => { if (pulseTimeout) clearTimeout(pulseTimeout) })`. Import onUnmounted from vue.

findings.title: handleToggle scrollIntoView fires on null ref when showClosed transitions true
findings.why_it_matters: handleToggle emits toggle-show-closed and then awaits nextTick before calling closedGroupRef.value?.scrollIntoView. The optional-chaining operator (?.) means no crash occurs, but the scroll simply does nothing silently. The root cause: the closed group div is rendered via `v-if='showClosed'` (KanbanBoard.vue line 144). When the user taps the sticky 'X closed' banner, handleToggle runs, emits the event, and awaits one tick. The parent (App.vue) receives the event, calls toggleShowClosed, which flips showClosed.value. This triggers a re-render of KanbanBoard's template. However, because handleToggle is running in KanbanBoard (child), the parent's reactive update and subsequent prop propagation back down to the child may not have flushed within a single nextTick. Vue's scheduler batches updates, and a cross-component reactive flush may require two ticks (parent flush + child vdom patch). The :ref='bindClosedRef' callback on the v-if div runs during the child's patch, which may be one tick after the nextTick awaited in handleToggle. Therefore closedGroupRef.value may still be null when scrollIntoView is called.
findings.evidence: KanbanBoard.vue line 144: `v-if='showClosed'`. Line 307-311: handleToggle awaits nextTick then calls closedGroupRef.value?.scrollIntoView. closedGroupRef is set by the template ref callback bindClosedRef (line 303-305), which only fires after the v-if renders the element. showClosed is a prop from App.vue, updated asynchronously via the emitted event.
findings.suggested_fix: Use a watch inside KanbanBoard to react to `props.showClosed` becoming true, then scroll in the watcher's callback after nextTick: `watch(() => props.showClosed, async (val) => { if (val) { await nextTick(); closedGroupRef.value?.scrollIntoView({ behavior: 'smooth' }) } })`. Remove the async/await from handleToggle and make it a simple emit.

findings.title: showClosed watcher closes panel before list re-renders — brief flash of stale panel content
findings.why_it_matters: The watcher at line 214 sets showPanel.value = false synchronously when showClosed becomes false and the panel's current app is terminal. Vue will batch this update with the showClosed change and flush them together. The ApplicationPanel uses v-if='showPanel' so it should disappear in the same render tick as the filter update. In practice, because showPanel = false runs in a watch callback (which runs before the render flush), the panel is removed before the filtered list renders. This should not cause a visible flash. However, if ApplicationPanel has its own leave transition, the component begins unmounting while displayApplications still contains the terminal app (the computed hasn't re-evaluated yet against the new showClosed value). This means a leaving panel transition would run against a list that still includes the app, which is correct. Risk is minimal but worth a visual verification.
findings.evidence: App.vue line 214-218: watch(showClosed, ...) sets showPanel.value = false. App.vue line 135: ApplicationPanel uses v-if='showPanel'. No leave transition observed on ApplicationPanel in the template.
findings.suggested_fix: Low priority. If a leave transition is ever added to ApplicationPanel, ensure the displayApplications filter is not applied until after the transition completes, or defer the panel close with nextTick.

findings.title: recentClosed/olderClosed are inconsistent with columns.accepted/columns.rejected — dual source of truth for closed apps in KanbanBoard
findings.why_it_matters: The watch at line 268-279 populates four data structures from props.applications: columns.accepted, columns.rejected, recentClosed, and olderClosed. recentClosed and olderClosed together contain exactly the same elements as columns.accepted + columns.rejected (just partitioned by age). However, the desktop Closed column uses recentClosed/olderClosed as draggable v-models, while the mobile Closed group uses columns.accepted/columns.rejected. If vuedraggable mutates recentClosed (e.g. splices out an element during a drag), columns.accepted/columns.rejected are NOT updated. The mobile column count badge (groupCount at line 281-283) reads from columns.accepted.length + columns.rejected.length — which will then show a stale count on mobile if the desktop column mutated recentClosed. Conversely, a drag on mobile mutates columns.accepted or columns.rejected but not recentClosed/olderClosed. This dual-source inconsistency means the closed column count can be wrong after a drag in either context.
findings.evidence: KanbanBoard.vue lines 272-278: watch populates both columns.accepted/rejected AND recentClosed/olderClosed from the same apps array. Line 281-283: groupCount reads from columns[s.value].length. Desktop draggables (lines 61, 83) use recentClosed/olderClosed. Mobile draggables (lines 167-178) use columns[stage.value].
findings.suggested_fix: Use a single source of truth for closed apps. Option A: drop recentClosed/olderClosed refs and derive them as computed properties from columns.accepted and columns.rejected (read-only slices), using a single closedAll ref as the v-model and keeping the partition purely visual. Option B: use columns.accepted and columns.rejected as the only v-models everywhere and add a computed closedRecent/closedOlder for display only (not as draggable v-models).

findings.title: partitionClosed uses rejected_at || accepted_at fallback: accepted apps with null accepted_at always sort to 'older'
findings.why_it_matters: partitionClosed resolves a date as `app.rejected_at || app.accepted_at || app.updated_at`. For an 'accepted' application: rejected_at is null/undefined, so accepted_at is used. If accepted_at is also null (the server sets it via PATCH /api/applications/:id/status, but older records or records advanced via the panel's direct field edits may not have it set), the fallback is updated_at. If updated_at is also null (possible for legacy data), date resolves to 0, and days = (now - 0) / 86_400_000 ≈ 20,000 days, placing the card firmly in 'older'. A recently accepted application with a null accepted_at would therefore be hidden under 'Show N older' immediately, making it invisible in the default view despite being new. The correct fallback order for an accepted app should be accepted_at || updated_at (skip rejected_at). For a rejected app: rejected_at || updated_at.
findings.evidence: KanbanBoard.vue line 256: `const dateStr = app.rejected_at || app.accepted_at || app.updated_at`. For a status='accepted' app: rejected_at is null by definition, so the expression evaluates accepted_at first. If accepted_at is null, updated_at is used — correct. However if all three are null, date=0 and days≈20000, placing the card in 'older' silently. For a status='rejected' app with a null rejected_at but a recent accepted_at (e.g. an app that was accepted then declined), the accepted_at date is used — potentially wrong.
findings.evidence_2: Server code not read; assumption based on schema description in CLAUDE.md: 'Each stage has a corresponding *_at date field on applications; PATCH /api/applications/:id/status auto-sets the relevant timestamp.' Direct field edits via the panel may not set the timestamp.
findings.suggested_fix: Use status-aware date resolution: `const dateStr = app.status === 'rejected' ? (app.rejected_at || app.updated_at) : (app.accepted_at || app.updated_at)`. This eliminates cross-contamination between rejected_at and accepted_at, and makes the fallback chain predictable.

findings.title: KanbanCard: quiet user_email row has identical text-ink-3 class in both branches — no visual distinction
findings.why_it_matters: The conditional class binding on the user_email line is `:class="['text-xs truncate mt-0.5', quiet ? 'text-ink-3' : 'text-ink-3']"`  — both branches evaluate to 'text-ink-3'. The quiet=true branch was presumably intended to use a lighter token (e.g. text-ink-4 or opacity-50) to visually distinguish the email on rejected cards, but the conditional is a no-op. This is a cosmetic bug but was clearly intentional in the diff (the original had a single static class; the new version added a ternary that does nothing).
findings.suggested_fix: Either remove the conditional entirely (static 'text-ink-3' suffices) or use a lighter token for the quiet state: `quiet ? 'text-ink-4 opacity-60' : 'text-ink-3'`. Same issue exists in TimelineView.vue line 52: `app.status === 'rejected' ? 'text-ink-3' : 'text-ink-3'`.

findings.title: TimelineView role_title span has identical text-ink-3 in both quiet/normal branches
findings.why_it_matters: Same copy-paste error as KanbanCard. The ternary `app.status === 'rejected' ? 'text-ink-3' : 'text-ink-3'` is a no-op. The role_title will render identically for rejected and non-rejected apps, defeating the visual quieting intent for role titles in the timeline view.
findings.evidence: TimelineView.vue line 52: both ternary branches return 'text-ink-3'.
findings.suggested_fix: Use `app.status === 'rejected' ? 'text-ink-3 opacity-60' : 'text-ink-3'` or define a dedicated quieter token.

residual_risks: If the server does not set rejected_at/accepted_at reliably (e.g. direct SQL edits, data migrations, or older records), partitionClosed will silently mis-classify cards as 'older' with no user-visible error.

The drag-to-closed interaction assigns 'rejected' for all non-terminal drags. If users discover they can drag to the closed column to archive, they will expect 'accepted' for certain cards and be surprised by the status change.

pulseGhost is triggered after the network round-trip completes (post-refreshApplication). If the network is slow (>1s), the ghost pulse fires noticeably late relative to the drag action, reducing its perceived connection to the event.

The mobile sticky banner uses `sticky top-0` inside a flex container. Depending on the parent scroll context, position:sticky may not work as expected — the scroll container is the inner overflow-x:auto flex row, not the page. This is a layout/CSS risk not covered by this review.
testing_gaps: No test for intra-closed drag (recentClosed to olderClosed) — verifying no spurious status-change is emitted.
No test for drag of an 'accepted' card into the closed column — currently silently reassigns to 'rejected'.
No test for pulseTimeout cleanup on HMR / component teardown.
No test for handleToggle scroll when closedGroupRef is null (the happy path is untestable without a real DOM and prop propagation timing).
No test for partitionClosed with all-null timestamps (accepted_at=null, rejected_at=null, updated_at=null).
No test for the show/hide toggle with an open panel whose app transitions to terminal status.
